content.problemStatement:
**This is the hard version of the problem. The only difference between this version and the easy version is that here, NNN and MMM can be as large as 10910^9109.**

Alice and Bob want to play a game on a grid with NNN rows and MMM columns.

The cell at the intersection of the iii-th row and jjj-th column is denoted (i,j)(i, j)(i,j).

Initially, all cells of the grid are colored white.

On a player's turn, the following happens:


First, the player must choose a cell (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) that is colored white.

If no such cell exists, the game ends immediately.
Then,

If it's Alice's turn, all cells that share a row or column with (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) will be colored **red.**
If it's Bob's turn, all cells that share a row or column with (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) will be colored **blue.**


Formally, all cells (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) such that x=ix = ix=i or y=jy = jy=j will be colored.

Note that even previously colored cells will have their colors overwritten to the new color.
You are given a cyclic string SSS of length KKK that contains only the characters `'A'` and `'B'`.

This string describes the sequence of which player will make which move.

That is, for any integer i≥1i \geq 1i≥1,


If S(i mod K)+1=AS_{(i\bmod K) + 1} = \texttt{A}S(imodK)+1​=A, Alice will make the iii-th move.
If S(i mod K)+1=BS_{(i\bmod K) + 1} = \texttt{B}S(imodK)+1​=B, Bob will make the iii-th move.
The winner of the game is the player with more cells of their color (red for Alice, blue for Bob).

If both players play optimally, find the winner - or decide that the game will end in a draw.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains three space-separated integers N,MN, MN,M and KKK — the dimensions of the grid, and the length of the string SSS.
The second line contains the string SSS of length KKK, whose characters are all `'A'` or `'B'`.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the result of the game:



`"Alice"`, if Alice will win.
`"Bob"`, if Bob will win.
`"Draw"`, if the game will end in a draw.

All strings must be output without the quotes.

Each character of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `Bob`, `BOB`, `bOb`, and `BoB` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: The sum of KKK over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: One way for the game to proceed is as follows:

S1=’A’S_1 = \text{'A'}S1​=’A’, so Alice moves first.
She can choose cell (1,2)(1, 2)(1,2), which will color the cells (1,2),(1,1),(2,2)(1, 2), (1, 1), (2, 2)(1,2),(1,1),(2,2) all red.
S2=’B’S_2 = \text{'B'}S2​=’B’, so the second move is Bob's.
The only uncolored cell is (2,1)(2, 1)(2,1), which he chooses.
This colors (2,1),(2,2),(1,1)(2, 1), (2, 2), (1, 1)(2,1),(2,2),(1,1) all blue, overwriting Alice's red color in the case of the latter two.
All the cells are colored, so the game ends.

Finally, there are three blue cells and one red cell, so Bob wins.
It can be shown that no matter what Alice does, she cannot win.
Test case 222: Every move is Alice's, so eventually she'll color all the cells red and win.
Test case 333: S="BA"S = \text{"BA"}S="BA", so the order of moves is Bob, Alice, Bob, Alice, Bob, Alice, ...
It can be shown that Bob has a winning strategy.
